content:
# Mark Chapter 16 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 41r–41v (PDF pages 82–83) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_016_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_016_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ When the Sabbath was past, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome bought spices, so that they might go and anoint him.

² Very early on the first day of the week, when the sun had risen, they went to the tomb.

³ They were saying to one another, "Who will roll away the stone for us from the entrance of the tomb?"

⁴ Looking up, they saw that the stone had been rolled back — it was very large.

⁵ Entering the tomb, they saw a young man sitting on the right side, dressed in a white robe, and they were alarmed.

⁶ He said to them, "Do not be alarmed. You seek Jesus of Nazareth, who was crucified. He has risen; he is not here. See the place where they laid him.

⁷ But go, tell his disciples and Peter that he is going before you to Galilee. There you will see him, just as he told you."

⁸ And they went out and fled from the tomb, for trembling and astonishment had seized them, and they said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid.

---

**[The Gospel of Mark ends here in Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus. Verses 9-20, the so-called "Longer Ending," do not appear in the earliest manuscripts and are not part of Mark's original composition.]**

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **The ending at 16:8:** The Gospel ends with the Greek word γάρ ("for") — an unprecedented ending for a Greek literary work. Whether Mark intended this ending or the original was lost remains one of the great unsolved questions of New Testament scholarship. The manuscript evidence decisively supports 16:8 as the ending of our earliest recoverable text.

2. **v. 5 — "a young man" (νεανίσκος):** Mark's restraint is characteristic. He does not say "angel." Matthew 28:2 makes the identification explicit. Mark's young man in a white robe at the tomb counterbalances the young man who fled naked at the arrest (14:51-52) — shame replaced by glory, flight replaced by proclamation.

3. **v. 7 — "and Peter":** The rehabilitation of Peter begins here. Despite his threefold denial, he is named specifically in the angel's message. This is grace compressed into two words.

4. **v. 8 — Fear and silence:** The women disobey the angel's command. They say nothing to anyone. Mark's Gospel ends in human failure — just as it has been characterized by human failure throughout. Yet the reader holds the Gospel in hand. The message was delivered. Someone spoke.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
